title: Why Your Body Keeps Score Even After Therapy
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: Therapy didn't fully heal your trauma? Learn why the body keeps the score and how somatic work with insights from Peter Levine can help you finally release stored survival stress.
imageDescription: Golden light illuminates the delicate, intricate network of a nervous system, visualized as golden circuitry. The surrounding space is warm, soft-focused, and peaceful, evoking a sense of healing and gentle repair from within.
htmlContent:
I've sat across from hundreds of people who describe exactly what you’re feeling right now—that strange, dissonant hum beneath the surface of a life that is supposed to be better by now. They have done the work, read the books, and spent countless hours in therapy dissecting the past, naming the wounds, and developing a sophisticated cognitive map of their own trauma, and yet, the body remains unconvinced. The chest still tightens in quiet moments, the jaw still clenches in traffic, and a sense of impending dread still descends without a clear trigger, a visceral reminder that understanding a threat is not the same as feeling safe from it. This is the great chasm in much of modern therapeutic work, the gap between the story the mind tells and the truth the body holds. We have been taught to believe that healing is a function of insight, that if we can just understand *why* we feel a certain way, the feeling itself will relent. But complex trauma doesn’t just leave a narrative scar; it leaves a physiological one. It rewires the nervous system, recalibrates its baseline for safety, and teaches it a language of threat that operates far below the threshold of conscious thought. The work of therapy can give us a brilliant translation of that language, but it often fails to teach us how to speak it back to the body in a way it can understand. The result is a frustrating and often shame-inducing stalemate where we know we are safe, but our body continues to brace for an impact that has already passed. The work, then, is not to argue with the body, but to learn its native tongue, which is the language of sensation, of impulse, of the deep, non-verbal currents that shape our entire experience of being alive.
You cannot think your way into a felt sense of safety. The body has its own logic.

The Echo in the Tissues

When we talk about the body keeping the score, we are not speaking in metaphor. We are describing a biological reality where unresolved survival responses remain stored in the nervous system as patterns of tension, collapse, and activation. Think of it like a computer program left running in the background, consuming processing power and subtly influencing the functioning of the entire system. You can change the desktop wallpaper, you can organize the files, but until you address the underlying process, the machine will continue to lag. Talk therapy is brilliant at helping us understand the program—when it was installed, what it was designed to do, how it interacts with other software. But it often lacks the tools to access the command line and end the process itself. At this juncture, the pioneering work of researchers like Peter Levine becomes so crucial. His development of Somatic Experiencing was born from a simple but profound observation of animals in the wild. After a life-threatening encounter, once the danger has passed, they don’t simply go back to grazing. They tremble, they shake, they take deep, spontaneous breaths—they physically discharge the immense energy that was mobilized for survival. Humans, with our sophisticated prefrontal cortex, have a tendency to interrupt this process. We rationalize, we suppress, we tell ourselves, "It's over, I'm fine," and in doing so, we trap that survival energy within the body. It becomes the echo in the tissues, the persistent hum of a threat that the conscious mind has long since filed away. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen countless individuals whose lives are still being run by this echo, a physiological ghost from a past they intellectually understand but have not yet somatically completed.

The Grammar of Sensation

To begin the work of somatic completion, we must first learn to read the body’s grammar, which is composed not of words or memories, but of pure sensation. This is a radical departure for most of us, who have been trained to privilege the cognitive narrative above all else. We are asked, “How do you feel about that?” far more often than we are asked, “What do you notice in your body right now?” The first question invites a story, an interpretation, a judgment. The second invites a direct, non-conceptual experience—a tightness in the throat, a warmth in the belly, a tingling in the hands. This is the raw data of the nervous system, the unedited feed from the front lines of your lived experience. Learning to track these sensations is like learning a new language. At first, it feels foreign and clumsy. The vocabulary is limited—"good," "bad," "uncomfortable." But with patient attention, the nuance begins to emerge. We start to notice the difference between the sharp, cold contraction of fear and the heavy, dense immobility of shutdown. We learn to sense the subtle energetic shift that precedes an emotional flashback, giving us a precious window of time to respond differently. This is not about analyzing the sensations or figuring out what they "mean" in a narrative sense. It is about staying with them, offering them our steady, non-judgmental presence, and allowing them to move, to shift, and to resolve on their own terms. It is a process of building trust with a part of ourselves we have long learned to ignore, override, or pathologize.
The nervous system doesn’t respond to what you believe. It responds to what it senses.
This is the core principle of moving from a top-down to a bottom-up approach to healing. We are not imposing a new belief on the body; we are creating the conditions for the body to have a new experience—an experience of safety, of completion, of release. It is a slow, deliberate, and deeply respectful process of re-establishing a connection that was severed as a profound act of survival. We are not fixing a flaw; we are coming home to a wisdom that has been waiting patiently for our return. For anyone who has felt stuck in a loop of repetitive emotional pain, learning this grammar can be the beginning of a genuine pathway toward resolution.

The Unfinished Conversation

One of the most critical aspects of Peter Levine’s work is the idea of completing the self-protective responses that were thwarted at the time of the trauma. When faced with an overwhelming threat, the body instinctively mobilizes for fight or flight. If neither of these options is possible or successful, it resorts to the most primitive survival strategy: freeze, or collapse. This is not a choice; it is a biological imperative, a function of the dorsal vagal part of our nervous system. The conscious mind may have been absent, but the body was there, and it prepared for an action it was never allowed to take. That thwarted impulse—the push that never happened, the scream that was never released, the legs that never ran—remains as an unfinished conversation in the nervous system. Somatic work creates a safe container to finally finish that conversation. This doesn’t mean re-enacting the trauma. Rather, it involves gently and slowly re-approaching the edge of that trapped energy and inviting the body to complete the impulse in a way that feels empowering now. It might be as small as feeling the muscles in the arms tense with a desire to push away, and allowing that tension to be there, to be felt, to be honored. It could be noticing the heat of anger in the torso and allowing it to rise without needing to direct it at anyone. A client once described this as feeling a "silent scream" in her throat that had been there for thirty years; our work together was simply to allow that sensation to be present, to give it space, until it eventually softened and resolved into a wave of profound grief, and then, finally, into a quiet stillness. This process, often called titration, is about touching into the traumatic activation in small, manageable doses, and then returning to a place of resource or safety in the body—a process called pendulation. It’s like gently rocking between the past and the present, allowing the nervous system to slowly digest the experience without becoming overwhelmed. It is a way of assuring the body, through direct experience, that the threat is over and that its brilliant survival strategies are no longer needed in the same way. This is essential for anyone struggling with the aftereffects of structural dissociation, where different parts of the self hold different pieces of the traumatic experience.

The Architecture of Return

As the nervous system begins to discharge this stored energy and complete these old survival loops, a new capacity for presence begins to emerge. This is not the forced, white-knuckled presence of a mindfulness exercise performed against a backdrop of internal chaos. It is an organic, bottom-up settling of the entire system. The world starts to look and feel different, not because the world has changed, but because the perceptual filter of trauma is beginning to dissolve. Colors may seem brighter, sounds may seem clearer, and a sense of spaciousness may begin to permeate moments that were once filled with anxiety. This is the return to the ventral vagal state, the neurological circuit of safety and social engagement that is our biological birthright. Re-inhabiting the body in this way can be both beautiful and disorienting. For those of us who have spent a lifetime living from the neck up, feeling the body’s aliveness can be a strange and sometimes frightening experience. It requires a willingness to be with sensation without a story, to feel energy move without needing to control it, and to trust the body’s innate intelligence. It is a journey from a life defined by reaction to a life guided by response. We begin to notice the gap between a trigger and our habitual reaction, and in that gap, we discover a new freedom. The work of organizations like the International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation (ISST-D) provides a clinical framework for this, but the lived experience is one of profound personal reclamation.
There is no version of growth that doesn't involve the dissolution of something you thought was permanent.
Ultimately, this journey is about more than just symptom reduction. It is about reclaiming the fullness of our human experience. It is about having access to our anger, our grief, our joy, and our vitality without being overwhelmed by them. It is about building a nervous system that is resilient and flexible, one that can move through the natural cycles of activation and settling with ease and grace. It is about discovering that the body is not a battlefield or a liability, but a source of profound wisdom, a faithful companion, and a gateway to a more vibrant and connected existence. This is the promise of somatic healing—not to erase the past, but to finally, truly, let it be over.

From Witnessing to Wholeness

One of the most profound shifts that occurs in this work is the movement from identifying with the traumatized parts of ourselves to holding them in a larger field of compassionate awareness. We stop being the anxiety, the shame, or the fear, and we become the witness to it. This is not a detached, intellectual witnessing, but an embodied presence that is warm, spacious, and unshakable. We learn to offer ourselves the very thing we needed most at the time of the original trauma: a regulated, attuned, and loving presence. In doing so, we become our own source of safety, our own secure attachment. This is the heart of what it means to heal from complex trauma, a topic explored in depth in articles on healing the fragmented self. This shift is not an event; it is a gradual unfolding. It is the slow, patient work of turning toward the parts of ourselves we have exiled, the sensations we have numbed, and the truths we have buried. It is the practice of meeting the raw, visceral reality of our inner world with curiosity instead of judgment. With every moment that we can stay present with an uncomfortable sensation without trying to fix it or flee from it, we are rewriting the deepest code of our nervous system. We are teaching it, through direct experience, that it can handle the intensity of our own aliveness. We are proving to it that the world inside is just as safe as the world outside. This is the ultimate act of self-love, not as a sentiment, but as a practice. It is the commitment to stay with ourselves, no matter what arises. It is the recognition that our symptoms are not signs that we are broken, but signals that we are striving toward wholeness. They are the language of a system that is intelligently, desperately trying to heal. Our job is not to silence the signals, but to learn to understand them, to honor their wisdom, and to provide the conditions they need to finally come to rest.
You are not a problem to be solved. You are a process to be witnessed.
This is the tender truth at the heart of somatic healing. It releases us from the exhausting and endless project of self-improvement and invites us into the gentle, moment-by-moment practice of self-witnessing. It is from this ground of embodied presence that a new life can finally, organically, begin to grow—one that is not free from pain, but is free from the prison of the past.

The comfortable ending would be to tell you that everything will be fine. But that would be a lie dressed up as kindness. The truth is that this work is hard, it takes longer than you want, and nobody can guarantee the outcome. Do it anyway. The alternative is worse.

Frequently Asked Questions

How is this different from traditional talk therapy?

Traditional talk therapy primarily engages the neocortex—the thinking, reasoning part of the brain. It helps you understand your story and change your beliefs. Somatic work, on the other hand, directly engages the brainstem and limbic system, the parts of the brain that govern survival responses and emotions. It bypasses the cognitive narrative to work directly with the body's stored sensations and impulses, aiming to complete physiological processes that talk therapy may not access.

Is it safe to explore these sensations on my own?

While gently noticing your body's sensations is generally safe, the process of intentionally contacting and releasing traumatic energy can be overwhelming without skilled support. It is highly recommended to work with a trained somatic therapist who can help you navigate the experience without becoming re-traumatized. A good practitioner helps create a safe container, co-regulates your nervous system, and guides you to touch into difficult sensations in manageable doses.

What's a simple first step to start connecting with my body?

Begin by simply noticing without judgment. A few times a day, pause and ask, "What is one sensation I can feel in my body right now?" It might be the feeling of your feet on the floor, the air on your skin, or a subtle tension in your shoulders. You don't need to name it, fix it, or understand it. The goal is simply to let your attention rest on the raw, physical sensation for a few breaths, gently building the muscle of interoception—the ability to sense the internal state of your body.

This path of somatic return is, in its essence, an act of profound kindness. It is the quiet, unwavering commitment to finally giving our body the compassionate attention it has always deserved. We learn to listen not to fix, but to understand; we learn to stay not to conquer, but to accompany. This is the deep, slow, and sacred work of coming home to ourselves, a journey beautifully articulated in the contemplative paths of teachers who explore the nature of consciousness and embodied healing. It is here, in the quiet landscape of our own physical being, that the truest and most lasting peace is found.
The body has a grammar. Most of us never learned to read it.
